en.extract:
The curve-billed thrasher (Toxostoma curvirostre) is a medium-sized mimid native to most of Mexico and to the deserts of southwestern United States. It is a non-migratory species, and throughout most of its range it is the most common desert thrasher. Several subspecies have been classified since 1827, though there is no consensus on the number. Allopatric speciation is believed to have played a major role in the variations of the curve-billed. It is grey-brown overall with a slightly curved bill, and is similar in appearance to the related Bendire's thrasher. It generally resides in desert regions of the United States and Mexico, but can inhabit areas predominately populated by humans.
The demeanor of the curve-billed has been described as "shy and rather wild", but it allows humans to view it closely. It is very aggressive in driving out potential threats, whether competitors for food or predators of its chicks. The curve-billed thrasher sometimes mimics several other species, though not to the extent of other mimics. It has a variety of distinctive songs, and this extensive repertoire of melodies has led it to be known as cuicacoche (songbird) in Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics

The species was first described as Toxostoma curvirostre by William Swainson in 1827. Since then, six subspecies have been recognized, and have been divided into two branches. Genetic research has indicated that the curve-billed thrasher is the sister species of a clade consisting of the ocellated thrasher, brown thrasher, long-billed thrasher, and Cozumel thrasher. This contrasts with an earlier suggestion that curve-billed and ocellated thrashers are closest relatives.


Subspecies
Eastern:

Toxostoma curvirostre oberholseri (Law, 1928) – Brownsville thrasher. Generally ranges from SE Texas to NE Mexico (E Coahuila, Nuevo León and Tamaulipas).
Toxostoma curvirostre curvirostre (Swainson, 1827) – Ranges from Central to South-Central Mexico (towards Puebla, Oaxaca, and Veracruz).
Toxostoma curvirostre celsum (R. T. Moore, 1941) – plateau thrasher. Range spans from SE Colorado, SW Kansas, extreme NW Oklahoma to S/SE Arizona, S New Mexico and W Texas to Northern Mexico (E Chihuahua to Guanajuato and Jalisco).
Western:

Toxostoma curvirostre insularum (van Rossem, 1930) – San Esteban thrasher. Located in the Islands of San Esteban and Tiburón off the coast of Sonora in the Gulf of California.
Toxostoma curvirostre maculatum (Nelson, 1900) – spotted thrasher. Found in NW Mexico (S Sonora, N Sinaloa, SW Chihuahua).
Toxostoma curvirostre occidentale (Ridgway, 1882) – Mazatlan thrasher. Located in Western Mexico (from Sinaloa and Nayarit to Jalisco).
Toxostoma curvirostre palmeri (Coues, 1872) – Palmer's thrasher. Range is S Arizona, N Sorona and Chihuahua. This species is the representative of the western species, and was suggested to have enough variations to be considered a different species from T.c. curvirostre. T.c. palmeri was also used in a 2009 proposal by the American Ornithologists' Union to be elevated to species status, along with the eastern T.c. curvisrostre representing the eastern curve-billed thrashers.


Description

The curve-billed thrasher is immediately recognized as a thrasher by its long tail and short wings. It is also recognized for its sickle-shaped bill, almost as long as its head width and brownish black in color. The body is compact with a large head, short wings and long tail. However, the tail is short relative to other thrasher species. The chest is grayish brown with circular brown-gray spots. The eyes are generally orange, shading to golden in adulthood. Juveniles are lacking in pale tips, rectrices, abdominal feathers are unkempt and upper parts and chest are washed rufously.
Variations are markedly different with each subspecies. The eastern subspecies has more distinct spots on its chest, more white along the tips and rectrices and obvious wing bars. T.c. palmeri has less spotting on chest and tips and less obvious wing bars. Other examples include T.c. curvirostre possessing longer wings and a tail than T.c. oberholseri; T.c. insularum is paler with more grey than T.c. palmeri and has more visible spotting on its breast.
One study published in 2003 suggested that curve-billed speciation was due to climate, which could be explained by molt and feather wear. The same study suggested that the Sierra Madre Occidental acts as a barrier to initiate differentiation within the species.
Measurements:

Length: 10.6–11.0 in (27–28 cm)
Weight: 2.1–3.3 oz (60–94 g)
Wingspan: 13.4–13.6 in (34–35 cm)


Voice and song
The most distinctive voice of the curve-billed is an abrupt and brash whit-wheet, which sounds akin to a person whistling to get one's attention. It is usually uttered from high perches. The songs are similar to that of the northern mockingbird but notes do not end as abruptly; its voice is regarded as more pleasant. The curve-billed can utter songs consecutively two or three times, the length spanning from 2 to 15 seconds. Some phrases are nasal and buzzy; its large vocabulary enables it to conjure successive songs that are usually different from one another.
As a mimid it is capable of mimicking several species, including scissor-tailed flycatcher, northern cardinal, pyrrhuloxia and, in one instance, a black-headed grosbeak.


Similar species
Because its coloration is similar to Bendire's thrasher, the two birds are easily mistaken for one another. The bill of the curve-billed is all black, while that of Bendire's is paler down to the lower mandible, showing a dark grey with a basal area that does not feature a stark contrast. The upper and lower mandibles of the curve-billed are curved, while the upper is curved and lower is essentially straight for the Bendires'. Although geographic variances for the curve-billed can make discerning breast patterns difficult, the curve-billed has a tendency to display larger and rounder spots overall on its breast, in contrast to the Bendires' smaller, more pronounced markings shaped like arrowheads. However, curve-billed thrashers can also present this feature. Bendires' typically have yellow eyes while curve-billed eyes are typically orange, but Bendires' can have variations that appear yellow-orange while some curve-billed eyes are red-orange, yellow-orange, or yellow. The curve-billed is heavier and is more likely to inhabit suburban regions; Bendires' are likely to avoid dense vegetation such as mesquite and prefers open grassland with scattered yucca or around hedgerows in agricultural areas. The song call of Bendire's is said to be more melodic and continuous with a chuck, while the curve-billed is known for its whit-wheet calls.
Some of the aforementioned differences, such as streaks on the breast, bill shape and eye color are even more prominent in juveniles of the two species.


Habitat and distribution

The curve-billed thrasher is commonly found throughout the southwestern United States from Arizona's Sonoran Desert across New Mexico to west Texas, southeastern Colorado and southwestern Kansas as well as most of Mexico from the Sonoran-Chihuahuan Deserts and south through the Mexican Plateau into Central Tamaulipas, inland to Oaxaca, and on the coast of Sonora to Nayarit. Vagrants have been found in bordering states of its range, as far north as North Dakota, Alberta and Manitoba and as far east as Florida. Palmeri types have been spotted in Florida, while curvirostre have been found in Iowa on several occasions. Other than previously noted exceptions, the curve-billed is essentially non-vagrant.
In comparison with other desert thrashers, the curve-billed is not as particular with habitats and can be found from sea-level up to 3,000 m (9,800 ft). It generally resides where cholla and saguaro cacti, ocotillo, mesquites, palo verde, and creosote bushes are prevalent. It can also be located along woodland edges, piñon, dry desert bushland and areas within its region where cacti are present.  If there is an adequate amount of desert vegetation near human habitation, along with feeders, the curve-billed can adapt within these environments.


Behavior and ecology
The curve-billed thrasher can generally be found running rapidly from cover to cover or flying near ground level from bush to bush. However, this bird is not shy about being in the open. It is generally a forager on ground level but can be very aggressive in routing out potential competitors for food at feeders, such as the Inca dove and round-tailed ground squirrel.


Mating
One recorded case of courtship behavior involving curve-billed thrashers describes two males attacking each other vigorously and resorting to purring and hissings sounds when neither bird appeared to relent. The two males then puffed up their chests and strutted up and down in front of the female. They continued to alternate between purr and hiss and fighting one another until one triumphed. The victor flew towards the female and both chirped melodically before copulation.


Breeding

The curve-billed breeding season begins in February and reaches an apex between March and May; however, new fledglings have been recorded as late as August. Dates vary within its range due to temperature and rainfall. Nests are generally built at conspicuous locations, the cholla being favored. Other potential nesting sites range from mesquite, prickly pear or yucca to the occasional oak and pine trees. The nest is generally a deep cup but can be flat with a depression for the eggs. The outer layer consists of thorny twigs while the inner layer includes smooth sticks, roots, coarse and fine grasses, rootlets or hair.

Both sexes build the nest; the building periods can be as brief as three days to as long as four weeks. Egg count differs by location; the number has been as low as two and as high as five. Egg color varies; they can appear bluish green to pale yellowish blue, spotted abundantly in reddish brown. Both sexes incubate but the female is mainly responsible as well as being the sole provider of brooding. Curve-billed parents defend their nests fervently against other species; snakes are the most frequent predators of eggs and nestlings. Curved-billed Thrashers are rarely victims of nest parasitism by cowbirds. In situations where food is scarce, parents feed older fledglings first. The fledging period ranges from 11 to 18 days.


Feeding
The curve-billed thrasher is an omnivore. Its diet includes invertebrates such as beetles, moths, butterflies, arachnids, and snails. It also eats vegetable matter, and fruits from cacti, prickly pear, hackberries, and anacua, among other plants. The curve-billed has also been spotted eating dog food, and will feed it to their chicks. They are ground feeders, and not well adapting to climbing on branches. Thrashers will take surface prey, but will also dig holes more than 2 inches (5.1 cm) deep to search for food. Curve-billeds will also sip nectar from saguaro blossoms and eat insects trapped within, serving as pollinators in the process. They also have great love for the resulting saguaro fruits, which is an important source of water in dry months.:168-187


Threats
Predators, which mainly focus on young birds, include snakes, coyotes, and roadrunners. This may account for very low brood success in the species, as only 20% of nests are successful in a year.:183-185
Curve-billed thrashers share a very similar range to the cactus wren, as well as a favorite species to nest in: the jumping cholla. Because of this, interspecific conflict is frequent. Fights over food are rare, but fights to protect fledglings are heated. They will vigorously work to destroy each other's nests, although typically only roosting nests, not breeding nests, are destroyed. Despite this, nests of curve-billeds and cactus wrens may still be concurrently and successfully raised even feet away from each other. Anderson & Anderson noted a minimum nest distance of a highly unusual 15 centimetres (neither nest was destroyed by the either throughout the entire season), although average interspecies nest distances were well over thirty metres. Nest destruction is almost always unsuccessful, and less intense, during breeding times, as both species adamantly defend their own nests. Once breeding season wanes, and fledglings emerge, competition becomes more fierce.:168-187


Status
Although its populations is declining, the curve-billed thrasher is rated as a species of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature.


References


External links

Curve-billed Thrasher Species Account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Curve-billed Thrasher - Toxostoma curvirostre - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
Curve-billed Thrasher photo gallery VIREO
"Toxostoma curvirostre". Integrated Taxonomic Information System.

The '''curve-billed thrasher''' ('''''Toxostoma curvirostre''''') is a medium-sized [[Mimidae|mimid]] native to most of [[Mexico]] and to the deserts of [[southwestern United States]]. It is a non-migratory species, and throughout most of its range it is the most common desert [[Toxostoma|thrasher]].<ref name="Dunne"/> Several subspecies have been classified since 1827, though there is no consensus on the number. [[Allopatric speciation]] is believed to have played a major role in the variations of the curve-billed.{{citation needed|date=May 2024}} It is grey-brown overall with a slightly curved bill, and is similar in appearance to the related [[Bendire's thrasher]]. It generally resides in desert regions of the United States and Mexico, but can inhabit areas predominately populated by humans.

The demeanor of the curve-billed has been described as "shy and rather wild", but it allows humans to view it closely.<ref name="TAMU"/> It is very aggressive in driving out potential threats, whether competitors for food or predators of its chicks. The curve-billed thrasher sometimes mimics several other species, though not to the extent of other mimics. It has a variety of distinctive songs, and this extensive repertoire of melodies has led it to be known as ''cuicacoche'' (songbird) in Mexico.<ref name="Davis"/>

==Taxonomy and systematics==
[[File:Curve-billedThrasher-28APR2017.jpg|alt=Bird in a cactus|right|thumb|258x258px|Atop a [[cholla cactus]] in [[Kingman, Arizona]].]]
The species was first [[Species description|described]] as ''Toxostoma curvirostre'' by [[William Swainson]] in 1827. Since then, six subspecies have been recognized, and have been divided into two branches.<ref name="Brewer14">{{cite book|last=Brewer|first=David|title=Wrens, Dippers, and Thrashers|publisher=Yale University Press|year=2001|page=115|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-r35SXfm9BYC&q=Curve-billed+Thrasher&pg=PT339|isbn=978-0-300-09059-8}}</ref> Genetic research has indicated that the curve-billed thrasher is the [[sister species]] of a [[clade]] consisting of the [[ocellated thrasher]], [[brown thrasher]], [[long-billed thrasher]], and [[Cozumel thrasher]].<ref name="Lovette2012">{{Cite journal|last1=Lovette |first1=I. J. |last2=Arbogast |first2=B. S. |last3=Curry |first3=R. L. |last4=Zink |first4=R. M. |last5=Botero |first5=C. A. |last6=Sullivan |first6=J. P. |last7=Talaba |first7=A. L. |last8=Harris |first8=R. B. |last9=Rubenstein |first9=D. R. |last10=Ricklefs |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.07.009 |first10=R. E. |last11=Bermingham |first11=E. |title=Phylogenetic relationships of the mockingbirds and thrashers (Aves: Mimidae) |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=63 |issue=2 |pages=219–229 |year=2012 |pmid=21867766 |bibcode=2012MolPE..63..219L |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dr2497/PUBLICATIONS_files/Lovette2012,%20MPE.pdf |archive-url=https://wayback.archive-it.org/all/20120712153809/http://www.columbia.edu/~dr2497/PUBLICATIONS_files/Lovette2012,%20MPE.pdf |archive-date=2012-07-12 }}</ref> This contrasts with an earlier suggestion that curve-billed and ocellated thrashers are closest relatives.<ref name = "Lovette2012" />

===Subspecies===
'''Eastern''':

* ''Toxostoma curvirostre oberholseri'' <small>(Law, 1928)</small> {{En dash}} '''Brownsville thrasher'''. Generally ranges from SE [[Texas]] to NE [[Mexico]] (E [[Coahuila]], [[Nuevo León]] and [[Tamaulipas]]).<ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)">{{cite web|title=Curve-billed Thrasher(''Toxostoma curvirostre'')|url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/curve-billed-thrasher-toxostoma-curvirostre|publisher=Internet Bird Collection|access-date=9 May 2014}}</ref><ref name="Avibase">{{cite web|title=Curve-billed Thrasher (''Toxostoma curvirostre'') (Swainson, 1827)|url=http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=033B53E6&sec=summary&ssver=1|publisher=AviBase: The World Bird Database|access-date=9 May 2014}}</ref>
* ''Toxostoma curvirostre curvirostre'' <small>(Swainson, 1827) {{En dash}}</small> Ranges from Central to South-Central Mexico (towards [[Puebla]], [[Oaxaca]], and [[Veracruz]]).<ref name="Brewer14" /><ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)" />
* ''Toxostoma curvirostre celsum'' <small>([[Robert Thomas Moore|R. T. Moore]], 1941)</small> {{En dash}} '''plateau thrasher'''. Range spans from SE [[Colorado]], SW [[Kansas]], extreme NW [[Oklahoma]] to S/SE [[Arizona]], S [[New Mexico]] and W Texas to Northern Mexico (E [[Chihuahua (state)|Chihuahua]] to [[Guanajuato]] and [[Jalisco]]).<ref name="Brewer14" /><ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)" />
'''Western''':

* ''Toxostoma curvirostre insularum'' <small>([[Adriaan Joseph van Rossem|van Rossem]], 1930) {{En dash}}</small> '''San Esteban thrasher'''. Located in the Islands of [[San Esteban Island|San Esteban]] and [[Tiburón Island|Tiburón]] off the coast of [[Sonora]] in the [[Gulf of California]].<ref name="Brewer14" /><ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)" />
* ''Toxostoma curvirostre maculatum'' <small>([[Thomas Hudson Nelson|Nelson]], 1900)</small> {{En dash}} '''spotted thrasher'''. Found in NW Mexico (S Sonora, N [[Sinaloa]], SW Chihuahua).<ref name="Brewer14" /><ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)" />
* ''Toxostoma curvirostre occidentale'' <small>([[Robert Ridgway|Ridgway]], 1882)</small> {{En dash}} '''Mazatlan thrasher'''. Located in Western Mexico (from Sinaloa and [[Nayarit]] to Jalisco).<ref name="Brewer14" /><ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)" />
* ''Toxostoma curvirostre palmeri'' <small>([[Elliott Coues|Coues]], 1872)</small> {{En dash}} '''Palmer's thrasher'''. Range is S Arizona, N Sorona and Chihuahua.<ref name="Brewer14" /><ref name="Toxostoma curvirostre (T. curvirostre)" /> This species is the representative of the western species, and was suggested to have enough variations to be considered a different species from ''T.c. curvirostre''.<ref name="Auk2003">{{Cite journal | doi = 10.1642/0004-8038(2003)120[0311:GVOTCT]2.0.CO;2| title = Geographic Variation of the Curve-Billed Thrasher (Toxostoma Curvirostre) Complex| journal = The Auk| volume = 120| issue = 2| page = 311| year = 2003| last1 = Rojas-Soto | first1 = O. R. | s2cid = 85706382}}</ref> ''T.c. palmeri'' was also used in a 2009 proposal by the [[American Ornithologists' Union]] to be elevated to species status, along with the eastern ''T.c. curvisrostre'' representing the eastern curve-billed thrashers.<ref name="AOU">{{cite web|title=AOU Committee on Classification and Nomenclature (North & Middle America): Votes on Proposals 2009-D |url=http://www.aou.org/committees/nacc/proposals/2009_D_votes_web.php|year=2009|publisher=American Ornithologists' Union|access-date=9 May 2014}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Curve-billed thrasher (Toxostoma curvirostre) in Picacho Hills, Las Cruces, New Mexico, USA.jpg|On the ground in [[Las Cruces, New Mexico]]|right|thumb]]
The curve-billed thrasher is immediately recognized as a thrasher by its long tail and short wings.<ref name="Brewer14"/> It is also recognized for its sickle-shaped bill, almost as long as its head width and brownish black in color. The body is compact with a large head, short wings and long tail. However, the tail is short relative to other thrasher species.<ref name="Dunne">{{cite book|last=Dunne|first=Pete|title=Pete Dunne's Essential Field Guide Companion: A Comprehensive Resource for Identifying North American Birds|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=2006|page=512|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ISgKT2ehj7oC&pg=PA512|isbn=978-0-300-09059-8}}</ref> The chest is grayish brown with circular brown-gray spots.<ref name="Brewer14"/> The eyes are generally orange, shading to golden in adulthood.<ref name="Dunne"/> Juveniles are lacking in pale tips, [[rectrices]], abdominal feathers are unkempt and upper parts and chest are washed rufously.<ref name="Brewer14"/>

Variations are markedly different with each subspecies. The eastern subspecies has more distinct spots on its chest, more white along the tips and rectrices and obvious wing bars. ''T.c. palmeri'' has less spotting on chest and tips and less obvious wing bars. Other examples include ''T.c. curvirostre'' possessing longer wings and a tail than ''T.c. oberholseri''; ''T.c. insularum'' is paler with more grey than ''T.c. palmeri'' and has more visible spotting on its breast.<ref name="Brewer14"/>

One study published in 2003 suggested that curve-billed [[speciation]] was due to climate, which could be explained by molt and feather wear. The same study suggested that the [[Sierra Madre Occidental]] acts as a barrier to initiate differentiation within the species.<ref name="Auk2003"/>

'''Measurements''':<ref>{{Cite web|title=Curve-billed Thrasher Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Curve-billed_Thrasher/id|access-date=2020-09-29|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref>

* '''Length''': {{convert|10.6|-|11.0|in|cm|abbr=on}}
* '''Weight''': {{convert|2.1|-|3.3|oz|g|abbr=on}}
* '''Wingspan''': {{convert|13.4|-|13.6|in|cm|abbr=on}}

===Voice and song===
The most distinctive voice of the curve-billed is an abrupt and brash ''whit-wheet'', which sounds akin to a person whistling to get one's attention.<ref name="Rylander">{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XNdW7C_RTGAC&pg=PA294|title=The Behavior of Texas Birds|last=Rylander|first=Kent|publisher=University of Texas Press|year=2002|isbn=978-0-292-77120-8|page=294}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetomexi00edwa|url-access=registration|title=A Field Guide to Mexican Birds: Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, El Salvador|last=Peterson|first=Roger Tory|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=1999|isbn=978-0-395-97514-5|page=[https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetomexi00edwa/page/180 180]}}</ref> It is usually uttered from high perches.<ref name="TAMU" /> The songs are similar to that of the [[northern mockingbird]] but notes do not end as abruptly; its voice is regarded as more pleasant.<ref name="Davis" /> The curve-billed can utter songs consecutively two or three times, the length spanning from 2 to 15 seconds.<ref name="Borror84">{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RQdxb1-i_kcC&pg=PA36|title=Songs of Western Birds|last=Borror|first=Donald J.|publisher=Dover Publications|year=1984|isbn=978-0-486-99913-5|page=36}}</ref> Some phrases are nasal and buzzy; its large vocabulary enables it to conjure successive songs that are usually different from one another.<ref name="Borror84" />

As a [[mimid]] it is capable of mimicking several species, including [[scissor-tailed flycatcher]], [[northern cardinal]], [[pyrrhuloxia]] and, in one instance, a [[black-headed grosbeak]].<ref name="TAMU" /><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.biomedsearch.com/article/Occasional-mimicry-night-time-singing/237732980.html|title=''Occasional mimicry and night-time singing by the Western Curve-billed Thrasher (Toxostoma curvirostre palmeri).''|last1=Johnson|first1=Roy R.|last2=Haight|first2=Lois T.|year=2010|work=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology|publisher=Wilson Ornithological Society|access-date=10 May 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140515084847/http://www.biomedsearch.com/article/Occasional-mimicry-night-time-singing/237732980.html|archive-date=15 May 2014}}</ref>

===Similar species===
Because its coloration is similar to Bendire's thrasher, the two birds are easily mistaken for one another. The bill of the curve-billed is all black, while that of Bendire's is paler down to the lower [[mandible]], showing a dark grey with a basal area that does not feature a stark contrast.<ref name="Peterson99">{{cite book|last=Peterson|first=Robert Tory|title=Advanced Birding|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=1999|pages=220–21, 223|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZVCprf8FF6kC&pg=PA220|isbn=978-0-395-97500-8}}</ref> The upper and lower mandibles of the curve-billed are curved, while the upper is curved and lower is essentially straight for the Bendires'.<ref name="Peterson99"/><ref name="Zimmer00"/> Although geographic variances for the curve-billed can make discerning breast patterns difficult, the curve-billed has a tendency to display larger and rounder spots overall on its breast, in contrast to the Bendires' smaller, more pronounced markings shaped like arrowheads.<ref name="Peterson99"/> However, curve-billed thrashers can also present this feature.<ref name="Peterson99"/> Bendires' typically have yellow eyes while curve-billed eyes are typically orange, but Bendires' can have variations that appear yellow-orange while some curve-billed eyes are red-orange, yellow-orange, or yellow.<ref name="Peterson99"/><ref name="Zimmer00">{{cite book|last=Zimmer|first=Kevin J.|title=Birding in the American West: A Handbook|publisher=Cornell University Press|year=2000|pages=236, 238–39|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TMtB8CG767EC&pg=PA236|isbn=978-0-8014-8328-8}}</ref> The curve-billed is heavier and is more likely to inhabit suburban regions; Bendires' are likely to avoid dense vegetation such as [[mesquite]] and prefers open grassland with scattered [[yucca]] or around hedgerows in agricultural areas.<ref name="Dunne"/><ref name="Peterson99"/> The song call of Bendire's is said to be more melodic and continuous with a ''chuck'', while the curve-billed is known for its ''whit-wheet'' calls.<ref name="Dunne"/>

Some of the aforementioned differences, such as streaks on the breast, bill shape and eye color are even more prominent in juveniles of the two species.<ref name="Peterson99"/><ref name="Zimmer00"/>

==Habitat and distribution==
[[File:Toxostoma curvirostre Arizona.jpg|thumb|300px|On a [[saguaro]], whose nearly open blooms will provide important food|alt=Bird on a cactus]]
The curve-billed thrasher is commonly found throughout the southwestern United States from Arizona's [[Sonoran Desert]] across New Mexico to west Texas, southeastern Colorado and southwestern Kansas as well as most of Mexico from the Sonoran-[[Chihuahuan Desert]]s and south through the [[Mexican Plateau]] into Central Tamaulipas, inland to Oaxaca, and on the coast of Sonora to Nayarit.<ref name="Dunne"/><ref name="Brewer14"/> Vagrants have been found in bordering states of its range, as far north as [[North Dakota]], [[Alberta]] and [[Manitoba]] and as far east as [[Florida]].<ref name="Brewer14"/> ''Palmeri'' types have been spotted in Florida, while ''curvirostre'' have been found in [[Iowa]] on several occasions.<ref name="Brewer14"/> Other than previously noted exceptions, the curve-billed is essentially non-vagrant.<ref name="Dunne"/>

In comparison with other desert thrashers, the curve-billed is not as particular with habitats and can be found from sea-level up to {{convert|3,000|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Brewer14"/> It generally resides where [[Cylindropuntia|cholla]] and [[saguaro]] cacti, [[ocotillo]], mesquites, [[Parkinsonia|palo verde]], and [[Larrea tridentata|creosote]] bushes are prevalent.<ref name="Davis">{{cite book|last= Davis|first=Barbara L.|title=A Field Guide to Birds of the Desert Southwest|publisher=Taylor Trade Publishing|year=1997|pages=228–29|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fRPyAAAAQBAJ&pg=PA228|isbn=978-0-88415-278-1}}</ref> It can also be located along woodland edges, [[pinyon pine|piñon]], dry desert bushland and areas within its region where cacti are present.<ref name="Dunne"/>  If there is an adequate amount of desert vegetation near human habitation, along with feeders, the curve-billed can adapt within these environments.<ref name="Dunne"/>

==Behavior and ecology==
The curve-billed thrasher can generally be found running rapidly from cover to cover or flying near ground level from bush to bush.<ref name="Brewer14" /> However, this bird is not shy about being in the open.<ref name="Dunne" /> It is generally a [[forage]]r on ground level but can be very aggressive in routing out potential competitors for food at feeders, such as the [[Inca dove]] and [[round-tailed ground squirrel]].<ref name="Davis" /><ref name="Brewer14" />

===Mating===
One recorded case of courtship behavior involving curve-billed thrashers describes two males attacking each other vigorously and resorting to purring and hissings sounds when neither bird appeared to relent. The two males then puffed up their chests and strutted up and down in front of the female. They continued to alternate between purr and hiss and fighting one another until one triumphed. The victor flew towards the female and both chirped melodically before copulation.<ref name="Davis"/>

===Breeding===
[[File:Toxostoma curvirostre -Tuscon, Arizona, USA -eggs in nest-8.jpg|thumb|Nest with eggs in a cactus, showing off the open cup construction|alt=Nest with four blue eggs]]
The curve-billed breeding season begins in February and reaches an apex between March and May; however, new fledglings have been recorded as late as August. Dates vary within its range due to temperature and rainfall.<ref name="TAMU">{{cite web|title=''Toxostoma curvirostre''|url=http://txtbba.tamu.edu/species-accounts/curve-billed-thrasher/|year=2000|work=Texas A&M AgriLifeExtension|publisher=Texas A&M University|access-date=15 May 2014}}</ref><ref name="Brewer14"/> Nests are generally built at conspicuous locations, the cholla being favored.<ref name="TAMU"/><ref name="Brewer14"/> Other potential nesting sites range from mesquite, prickly pear or yucca to the occasional [[oak trees|oak]] and [[pine trees]].<ref name="TAMU"/><ref name="Brewer14"/> The nest is generally a deep cup but can be flat with a depression for the eggs.<ref name="TAMU"/> The outer layer consists of thorny twigs while the inner layer includes smooth sticks, roots, coarse and fine grasses, rootlets or hair.<ref name="TAMU"/><ref name="Brewer14"/>

[[File:Curve-billedThrasherChicks.jpg|thumb|Curve-billed thrasher chicks in a nest constructed between branches of cholla cactus.|alt=Nest with chicks in a cactus]]
Both sexes build the nest; the building periods can be as brief as three days to as long as four weeks.<ref name="TAMU"/> Egg count differs by location; the number has been as low as two and as high as five.<ref name="Brewer14"/> Egg color varies; they can appear bluish green to pale yellowish blue, spotted abundantly in reddish brown.<ref name="Brewer14"/> Both sexes [[Egg incubation|incubate]] but the female is mainly responsible as well as being the sole provider of [[brooding]].<ref name="TAMU"/> Curve-billed parents defend their nests fervently against other species; [[snake]]s are the most frequent predators of eggs and nestlings. Curved-billed Thrashers are rarely victims of nest [[parasitism]] by [[cowbirds]].<ref name="TAMU"/><ref name="Brewer14"/> In situations where food is scarce, parents feed older fledglings first.<ref name="TAMU"/> The fledging period ranges from 11 to 18 days.<ref name="Brewer14"/>

===Feeding===
The curve-billed thrasher is an [[omnivore]]. Its diet includes invertebrates such as [[beetle]]s, [[moth]]s, [[butterflies]], [[arachnid]]s, and [[snail]]s.<ref name="TAMU"/><ref name="Brewer14"/> It also eats vegetable matter, and fruits from cacti, [[Opuntia|prickly pear]], [[Celtis|hackberries]], and [[anacua]], among other plants. The curve-billed has also been spotted eating dog food, and will feed it to their chicks.<ref name="TAMU"/> They are ground feeders, and not well adapting to climbing on branches. Thrashers will take surface prey, but will also dig holes more than {{convert|2|in|cm|abbr=}} deep to search for food. Curve-billeds will also sip nectar from [[saguaro]] blossoms and eat insects trapped within, serving as pollinators in the process. They also have great love for the resulting saguaro fruits, which is an important source of water in dry months.<ref name=":8" /><sup>:168-187</sup>

=== Threats ===
Predators, which mainly focus on young birds, include snakes, coyotes, and [[roadrunner]]s. This may account for very low brood success in the species, as only 20% of nests are successful in a year.<ref name=":8" /><sup>:183-185</sup>

Curve-billed thrashers share a very similar range to the [[cactus wren]], as well as a favorite species to nest in: the [[Cylindropuntia fulgida|jumping cholla]]. Because of this, [[Interspecific competition|interspecific conflict]] is frequent. Fights over food are rare, but fights to protect fledglings are heated. They will vigorously work to destroy each other's nests, although typically only roosting nests, not breeding nests, are destroyed. Despite this, nests of curve-billeds and cactus wrens may still be concurrently and successfully raised even feet away from each other. Anderson & Anderson noted a minimum nest distance of a highly unusual 15 centimetres (neither nest was destroyed by the either throughout the entire season), although average interspecies nest distances were well over thirty metres. Nest destruction is almost always unsuccessful, and less intense, during breeding times, as both species adamantly defend their own nests. Once breeding season wanes, and fledglings emerge, competition becomes more fierce.<ref name=":8">{{Cite book|title=The Cactus Wren|last1=Anderson|first1=Anders H.|last2=Anderson|first2=Anne|date=1972|publisher=University of Arizona Press|isbn=0-8165-0399-0|location=Tucson|oclc=578051}}</ref><sup>:168-187</sup>

== Status ==
Although its populations is declining, the curve-billed thrasher is rated as a species of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021"/>
